# SEO Rules

Full reference infrastructure lives in:
- `apps/web/src/lib/seo.ts` — siteConfig, 19 schema generators, `generatePageMetadata()`
- `apps/web/src/utils/seoUtils.ts` — blog/use-case metadata helpers
- `apps/web/src/lib/sitemapData.ts` — 11-part sitemap architecture
- `apps/web/src/i18n/getAlternates.ts` — hreflang helpers

---

## Page Titles

The root layout (`apps/web/src/app/layout.tsx`) defines:

```typescript
title: { template: `%s | GAIA` }
```

**Never include "GAIA" or "| GAIA" in a page's own title string** — it renders as `Page | GAIA | GAIA`.

```typescript
// Wrong — "Features — GAIA | GAIA"
export const metadata = { title: "Features — GAIA" };

// Wrong — "AI Chief of Staff | GAIA | GAIA"
export const metadata = { title: "AI Chief of Staff | GAIA" };

// Correct — "Features | GAIA"
export const metadata = { title: "Features" };

// Correct — "AI Chief of Staff — Your Proactive AI | GAIA"
export const metadata = { title: "AI Chief of Staff — Your Proactive AI" };
```

This applies to both static `export const metadata` objects and dynamic `generateMetadata()` functions — strip `| GAIA` from any returned title string.

**Title formatting:**
- Lead with the most descriptive keyword, not the brand name
- Use em-dash `—` (not pipe `|`) to separate primary topic from subtitle
- Keep under 60 characters where possible
- No trailing punctuation

---

## Meta Descriptions

- 120–155 characters
- Describe what the page *does*, not what the product *is*
- Do not repeat the title verbatim
- End with a benefit or differentiator

---

## Always Use `generatePageMetadata()` for New Pages

`lib/seo.ts` exports `generatePageMetadata()` — use it instead of building metadata objects by hand. It handles OG, Twitter, canonical, noIndex, article timestamps, and authors in one call:

```typescript
import { generatePageMetadata } from "@/lib/seo";

export async function generateMetadata(): Promise<Metadata> {
  return generatePageMetadata({
    title: "My Page Title",
    description: "What this page does in 120–155 chars.",
    path: "/my-page",
    keywords: ["relevant", "keyword", "list"],
  });
}
```

Never construct `openGraph` or `twitter` metadata blocks manually — `generatePageMetadata()` already does this correctly and consistently.

---

## Structured Data (JSON-LD)

`lib/seo.ts` exports 19 typed schema generators. Use them — never write raw JSON-LD objects inline.

| Need | Generator |
|---|---|
| Any new page | `generateWebPageSchema()` |
| FAQ section | `generateFAQSchema()` + render `<FAQAccordion>` |
| How-to / steps | `generateHowToSchema()` |
| Blog post | `generateArticleSchema()` |
| List of items | `generateItemListSchema()` |
| Glossary term | `generateDefinedTermSchema()` |
| Product/app | `generateProductSchema()` |
| Breadcrumbs | `generateBreadcrumbSchema()` |

Render schemas with `<JsonLd>` from `components/seo/JsonLd.tsx`. Every programmatic page should have at minimum a `WebPageSchema` and a `BreadcrumbSchema`.

```typescript
import JsonLd from "@/components/seo/JsonLd";
import { generateBreadcrumbSchema, generateWebPageSchema } from "@/lib/seo";

// In the page component (Server Component):
<JsonLd
  schema={[
    generateWebPageSchema({ title, description, url }),
    generateBreadcrumbSchema([
      { name: "Home", url: "/" },
      { name: "Features", url: "/features" },
      { name: feature.title, url: `/features/${slug}` },
    ]),
  ]}
/>
```

---

## Programmatic SEO Pages (`generateStaticParams` + `generateMetadata`)

Every `[slug]` route must implement both:

```typescript
export async function generateStaticParams() {
  const items = await fetchAllItems();
  return items.map((item) => ({ slug: item.slug }));
}

export async function generateMetadata({ params }): Promise<Metadata> {
  const item = await getItem(params.slug);
  if (!item) return {};
  return generatePageMetadata({
    title: item.title,
    description: item.description,
    path: `/section/${params.slug}`,
    keywords: [item.name, item.category, ...commonKeywords],
  });
}
```

**For integration/marketplace pages:**
- Use `revalidate = 60` (ISR) since content is API-driven
- Set `dynamicParams = true` to allow fallback rendering for new slugs
- Generate category-specific title and description templates (see `marketplace/[slug]/page.tsx` for the pattern)
- Always include a `SoftwareApplication` schema, HowTo schema, and FAQ schema

**Canonical URLs:**
- Use `getCanonicalUrl(path)` from `lib/seo.ts` — never construct URLs manually
- For near-duplicate pages (glossary terms, synonym slugs), set `canonicalPath` to the primary slug

---

## Hreflang / Internationalization

Translated pages (anything under `[locale]/`) that have content in all 7 locales must include `alternates.languages`:

```typescript
import { getAlternates } from "@/i18n/getAlternates";

export async function generateMetadata({ params }) {
  return {
    ...generatePageMetadata({ title, description, path }),
    alternates: {
      canonical: `/my-page`,
      languages: getAlternates("/my-page"),
    },
  };
}
```

**Translated (needs hreflang):** `/compare`, `/alternative-to`, `/automate`, `/for`, `/learn`, `/use-cases`

**Untranslated (English only — no hreflang):** `/marketplace`, `/blog`, `/download`, `/login`, `/signup`, `/terms`, `/privacy`

---

## Sitemaps

The sitemap system has 11 named segments (`lib/sitemapData.ts`). When adding new content types:

1. Add a new sitemap ID and handler in `sitemapData.ts`
2. Add the route to `app/sitemap/[id]/route.ts`
3. Set appropriate `priority` and `changefreq`:
   - Homepage: `1.0` / `daily`
   - Core feature/product pages: `0.9` / `weekly`
   - Blog posts: `0.8` / `weekly` (update `lastmod` from post date)
   - Static informational pages: `0.7–0.8` / `monthly`
   - Legal/auth: `0.4–0.6` / `monthly`
4. Use `withLocaleUrls()` for translated pages to include all language variants

---

## OG Images

Dynamic OG images live in `app/api/og/`. When adding a new section with programmatic pages:

1. Create `app/api/og/[section]/route.tsx` using `next/og` (edge runtime)
2. Reference it in `generateMetadata()`:
   ```typescript
   openGraph: {
     images: [`/api/og/my-section?slug=${params.slug}`],
   }
   ```
3. Always provide a 1200×630 fallback if the route fails
4. Preload the wallpaper and Inter font — see existing OG routes for the pattern

---

## FAQ Pages

Any page with an FAQ section should:
1. Render `<FAQAccordion faqs={feature.faqs} />` for the visible UI
2. Inject `generateFAQSchema(feature.faqs)` via `<JsonLd>` for rich snippets
3. Have at least 3 questions. Google won't show the rich snippet for fewer.

---

## noIndex

Use `generatePageMetadata({ noIndex: true })` for:
- Auth pages (`/login`, `/signup`, `/verify-email`)
- Internal/dashboard pages
- Thank-you / success pages
- Any page with `?q=` or pagination query params that duplicate content

Never set `noIndex` on public marketing or programmatic SEO pages.

input.description: Inspect failing triggers/schema log entries on 05-16

content.text: Found the smoking gun: the failures are **CORS preflight `OPTIONS /api/v1/triggers/schema` returning HTTP 400**, in bursts of 3 (React Query's default retries). A failed preflight makes the browser abort with a network error (no `response`), which is non-401 → toast fires, and the 3 retries produce repeated toasts. Let me check the backend CORS config to see why preflight 400s.

content.text: CORS uses `allow_headers=["*"]` and allows OPTIONS, so the 400 preflight points to a disallowed **origin**. Note the middleware order: `WorkOSAuthMiddleware` is added last (line 90), so it runs *before* `CORSMiddleware` (line 74). Let me check `get_allowed_origins()` and reproduce the preflight against the running backend.

"""
Middleware configuration for the GAIA FastAPI application.

This module provides functions to configure middleware for the FastAPI application.
"""

from app.config.settings import settings
from shared.py.wide_events import log as wide_log
from app.api.v1.middleware import (
    LoggingMiddleware,
    ProfilingMiddleware,
    WorkOSAuthMiddleware,
)
from app.api.v1.middleware.timeout import RequestTimeoutMiddleware
from app.api.v1.middleware.rate_limiter import limiter
from app.core.bot_auth_middleware import BotAuthMiddleware
from fastapi import FastAPI, Request
from fastapi.middleware.cors import CORSMiddleware
from fastapi.responses import JSONResponse
from slowapi.errors import RateLimitExceeded
from slowapi.middleware import SlowAPIMiddleware
from workos import AsyncWorkOSClient


async def rate_limit_handler(request: Request, exc: Exception) -> JSONResponse:
    """Handle rate limit exceeded exceptions."""
    wide_log.warning(
        "rate_limit_exceeded",
        client_ip=request.client.host
        if request.client
        else request.headers.get("x-forwarded-for", "unknown"),
        path=request.url.path,
        method=request.method,
        retry_after=getattr(exc, "retry_after", None),
    )
    return JSONResponse(
        status_code=429,
        content={
            "error": "rate_limit_exceeded",
            "detail": str(exc),
            "retry_after": getattr(exc, "retry_after", None),
        },
    )


def configure_middleware(app: FastAPI) -> None:
    """
    Configure middleware for the FastAPI application.

    Args:
        app (FastAPI): FastAPI application instance
    """

    # Attach limiter to app state
    app.state.limiter = limiter

    # Exception handler for rate limiting
    app.add_exception_handler(RateLimitExceeded, rate_limit_handler)

    # Add rate limiting middleware
    app.add_middleware(SlowAPIMiddleware)

    # Add pyinstrument profiling middleware for detailed call stack analysis
    app.add_middleware(ProfilingMiddleware)

    # Add logging middleware
    app.add_middleware(LoggingMiddleware)

    # Add request timeout middleware (inside CORS so CORS headers always present,
    # outside Logging so timeouts are logged)
    app.add_middleware(RequestTimeoutMiddleware)

    # Configure CORS
    app.add_middleware(
        CORSMiddleware,
        allow_origins=get_allowed_origins(),
        allow_origin_regex=get_allowed_origin_regex(),
        allow_credentials=True,
        allow_methods=["GET", "POST", "PUT", "DELETE", "PATCH", "OPTIONS"],
        allow_headers=["*"],
    )

    # Add bot authentication middleware (before WorkOS to allow bot auth to take precedence)
    app.add_middleware(BotAuthMiddleware)

    # Add WorkOS authentication middleware
    workos_client = AsyncWorkOSClient(
        api_key=settings.WORKOS_API_KEY, client_id=settings.WORKOS_CLIENT_ID
    )
    app.add_middleware(WorkOSAuthMiddleware, workos_client=workos_client)


def get_allowed_origins() -> list[str]:
    """
    Get allowed origins for CORS based on environment.

    Returns:
        list[str]: List of allowed origins
    """
    # Always include configured frontend URL
    allowed_origins = [settings.FRONTEND_URL]

    # Desktop app embedded Next.js server ports (5174 is preferred; 5175-5180
    # are fallbacks when the preferred port is already in use)
    desktop_origins = [f"http://localhost:{port}" for port in range(5174, 5181)]

    # Add additional origins based on environment
    if settings.ENV == "production":
        # Only allow trusted HTTPS origins in production
        allowed_origins.extend(
            [
                "https://heygaia.io",
                "https://www.heygaia.io",
                "https://heygaia.app",
                *desktop_origins,
            ]
        )
    else:
        # Allow development origins
        allowed_origins.extend(
            [
                "http://localhost:3000",
                "http://192.168.138.215:5173",
                "https://192.168.13.215:5173",
                *desktop_origins,
            ]
        )

    return allowed_origins


def get_allowed_origin_regex() -> str | None:
    """Regex of additional allowed origins.

    In development we allow any portless tunnel (``https://<slug>.localhost:<port>``)
    so dev URLs like ``https://voice-demo.localhost:1355`` reach the API without
    hardcoding each subdomain.
    """
    if settings.ENV == "production":
        return None
    return r"^https://[a-z0-9-]+\.localhost(?::\d+)?$"

content.text: CORS in dev allows `http://localhost:3000` and worktree-style `https://<slug>.localhost:<port>` origins, but NOT arbitrary `http://localhost:<other-port>`. Worktrunk assigns each worktree a non-3000 dev port. Let me reproduce preflights and check whether 05-16 broke globally or only for this route.

content.text:
Confirmed and reproduced. The mechanism is now certain:

- This worktree's web dev server runs on **`http://localhost:13335`** (`wt url`).
- CORS preflight `OPTIONS /triggers/schema` from `localhost:3000` → **200**, but from `localhost:13335` (or any other port) → **400**.
- On 05-16, *every* endpoint's preflight 400'd (conversations, integrations, payments, onboarding/personalization, triggers/schema) — a global CORS failure caused by the non-allowlisted dev origin.

Let me confirm the `FRONTEND_URL` default and what port the web server actually binds to in a worktree.

content.text:
Root cause fully confirmed. Here's what's happening:

**The bug is a CORS preflight failure, not a backend or frontend logic error.**

- Your dev server in this worktree runs on a worktrunk-assigned hash port (`wt url` → `http://localhost:13335`), not `http://localhost:3000`.
- Every `apiauth` request sends a custom `x-timezone` header with credentials, so the browser must send a CORS **preflight** `OPTIONS` first.
- The backend's dev CORS allowlist (`get_allowed_origins()` in `app/core/middleware.py`) only includes `http://localhost:3000` plus an `allow_origin_regex` that **requires `https://<slug>.localhost`**. Any other localhost port/scheme is rejected.
- Reproduced just now: `OPTIONS /triggers/schema` from `localhost:3000` → **200**, from `localhost:13335` → **400**.
- A 400 preflight makes the browser abort the real GET with a network error (no HTTP response). `apiService` only suppresses `401`s (service.ts:60), so this non-401 error shows the `errorMessage` toast. React Query retries the default 3×, so you get a burst of identical toasts.
- The 05-16 logs confirm this was global, not route-specific: `OPTIONS … 400` hit `conversations`, `integrations/*`, `payments/*`, `onboarding/personalization`, AND `triggers/schema` — all in retry-bursts of 3. You notice it in the workflows section because `WorkflowModal` (mounted by `OnboardingWorkflowCards`) fires `useTriggerSchemas()` on mount, and that toast isn't silenced.

The fix is to broaden the **dev-only** CORS origin regex so any localhost/127.0.0.1 origin (any port, http or https, with or without a `.localhost` subdomain) is accepted, which is what the worktree workflow needs. Production behavior is unchanged.

    so dev URLs like ``https://voice-demo.localhost:1355`` reach the API without
    hardcoding each subdomain.

def get_allowed_origin_regex() -> str | None:
    """Regex of additional allowed origins.

    In development we allow any ``localhost`` / ``127.0.0.1`` origin on any port,
    over http or https, with an optional ``<slug>.`` subdomain. This covers both
    the default dev server (``http://localhost:3000``) and worktree dev servers,
    which bind to per-branch hash ports (e.g. ``http://localhost:13335``), as well
    as subdomain tunnels like ``https://voice-demo.localhost:1355``. Without this,
    every credentialed request preflight (OPTIONS) is rejected with 400.
    """
    if settings.ENV == "production":
        return None
    return r"^https?://([a-z0-9-]+\.)?(localhost|127\.0\.0\.1)(?::\d+)?$"

"""
Middleware configuration for the GAIA FastAPI application.

This module provides functions to configure middleware for the FastAPI application.
"""

from app.config.settings import settings
from shared.py.wide_events import log as wide_log
from app.api.v1.middleware import (
    LoggingMiddleware,
    ProfilingMiddleware,
    WorkOSAuthMiddleware,
)
from app.api.v1.middleware.timeout import RequestTimeoutMiddleware
from app.api.v1.middleware.rate_limiter import limiter
from app.core.bot_auth_middleware import BotAuthMiddleware
from fastapi import FastAPI, Request
from fastapi.middleware.cors import CORSMiddleware
from fastapi.responses import JSONResponse
from slowapi.errors import RateLimitExceeded
from slowapi.middleware import SlowAPIMiddleware
from workos import AsyncWorkOSClient


async def rate_limit_handler(request: Request, exc: Exception) -> JSONResponse:
    """Handle rate limit exceeded exceptions."""
    wide_log.warning(
        "rate_limit_exceeded",
        client_ip=request.client.host
        if request.client
        else request.headers.get("x-forwarded-for", "unknown"),
        path=request.url.path,
        method=request.method,
        retry_after=getattr(exc, "retry_after", None),
    )
    return JSONResponse(
        status_code=429,
        content={
            "error": "rate_limit_exceeded",
            "detail": str(exc),
            "retry_after": getattr(exc, "retry_after", None),
        },
    )


def configure_middleware(app: FastAPI) -> None:
    """
    Configure middleware for the FastAPI application.

    Args:
        app (FastAPI): FastAPI application instance
    """

    # Attach limiter to app state
    app.state.limiter = limiter

    # Exception handler for rate limiting
    app.add_exception_handler(RateLimitExceeded, rate_limit_handler)

    # Add rate limiting middleware
    app.add_middleware(SlowAPIMiddleware)

    # Add pyinstrument profiling middleware for detailed call stack analysis
    app.add_middleware(ProfilingMiddleware)

    # Add logging middleware
    app.add_middleware(LoggingMiddleware)

    # Add request timeout middleware (inside CORS so CORS headers always present,
    # outside Logging so timeouts are logged)
    app.add_middleware(RequestTimeoutMiddleware)

    # Configure CORS
    app.add_middleware(
        CORSMiddleware,
        allow_origins=get_allowed_origins(),
        allow_origin_regex=get_allowed_origin_regex(),
        allow_credentials=True,
        allow_methods=["GET", "POST", "PUT", "DELETE", "PATCH", "OPTIONS"],
        allow_headers=["*"],
    )

    # Add bot authentication middleware (before WorkOS to allow bot auth to take precedence)
    app.add_middleware(BotAuthMiddleware)

    # Add WorkOS authentication middleware
    workos_client = AsyncWorkOSClient(
        api_key=settings.WORKOS_API_KEY, client_id=settings.WORKOS_CLIENT_ID
    )
    app.add_middleware(WorkOSAuthMiddleware, workos_client=workos_client)


def get_allowed_origins() -> list[str]:
    """
    Get allowed origins for CORS based on environment.

    Returns:
        list[str]: List of allowed origins
    """
    # Always include configured frontend URL
    allowed_origins = [settings.FRONTEND_URL]

    # Desktop app embedded Next.js server ports (5174 is preferred; 5175-5180
    # are fallbacks when the preferred port is already in use)
    desktop_origins = [f"http://localhost:{port}" for port in range(5174, 5181)]

    # Add additional origins based on environment
    if settings.ENV == "production":
        # Only allow trusted HTTPS origins in production
        allowed_origins.extend(
            [
                "https://heygaia.io",
                "https://www.heygaia.io",
                "https://heygaia.app",
                *desktop_origins,
            ]
        )
    else:
        # Allow development origins
        allowed_origins.extend(
            [
                "http://localhost:3000",
                "http://192.168.138.215:5173",
                "https://192.168.13.215:5173",
                *desktop_origins,
            ]
        )

    return allowed_origins


def get_allowed_origin_regex() -> str | None:
    """Regex of additional allowed origins.

    In development we allow any portless tunnel (``https://<slug>.localhost:<port>``)
    so dev URLs like ``https://voice-demo.localhost:1355`` reach the API without
    hardcoding each subdomain.
    """
    if settings.ENV == "production":
        return None
    return r"^https://[a-z0-9-]+\.localhost(?::\d+)?$"

content.text: All 4 trigger-schema hits in the log are my own curl tests — your browser's `GET /triggers/schema` genuinely never reaches the backend, even at 19:19 when you opened the modal. That means the browser is rejecting it client-side. A preflight can return 200 yet still *fail* if it lacks the `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` header. Let me dump the actual preflight response headers.

input.command: echo "=== OPTIONS preflight headers (Origin localhost:3000) ==="; curl -s -D - -o /dev/null -X OPTIONS "http://localhost:8000/api/v1/triggers/schema" -H "Origin: http://localhost:3000" -H "Access-Control-Request-Method: GET" -H "Access-Control-Request-Headers: x-timezone"; echo "=== compare: workflows path preflight ==="; curl -s -D - -o /dev/null -X OPTIONS "http://localhost:8000/api/v1/workflows/wf_test" -H "Origin: http://localhost:3000" -H "Access-Control-Request-Method: GET" -H "Access-Control-Request-Headers: x-timezone"

content.text:
`net::ERR_EMPTY_RESPONSE` is the real signal — the server accepts the TCP connection then closes it **without sending any response**. It's not CORS, and it's not specific to triggers/schema: `/integrations/status` and `/integrations/config` fail the same way, all firing concurrently. That's why none of them appear in the backend request log — no response was ever produced.

Let me gather evidence on what the server is doing at that moment rather than guess. Let me check how the API dev server runs (reload?), the timeout middleware, and recent shutdown/reload/crash events in the logs.

input.command: cd /Users/aryan/Projects/GAIA/gaia-onboarding-revamp && echo "=== how api dev runs (reload?) ==="; grep -rn "reload\|uvicorn\|fastapi dev\|--workers" apps/api/project.json package.json 2>/dev/null | head; echo "=== api dev command in project.json ==="; python3 -c "import json;d=json.load(open('apps/api/project.json'));import sys;t=d.get('targets',{});print(json.dumps({k:t[k].get('options',{}) for k in t if k in ('dev','serve')},indent=2))" 2>/dev/null | head -40
input.description: Check API dev server run config for reload/workers

content.text:
Confirmed. Here's what's actually happening — no assumptions, backed by the logs and your console output.

## Root cause: the API server is intermittently down (restarting), not a CORS or endpoint bug

Your console shows the real error:
```
:8000/api/v1/integrations/status   net::ERR_EMPTY_RESPONSE
:8000/api/v1/triggers/schema       net::ERR_EMPTY_RESPONSE
:8000/api/v1/integrations/config   net::ERR_EMPTY_RESPONSE
AxiosError: Network Error  (xhr handleError)
```

`ERR_EMPTY_RESPONSE` = the server accepted the TCP connection then closed it **without sending any response**. It hits three different endpoints at once, which rules out anything endpoint-specific.

The cause is the dev server's reload cycle:
- `apps/api/project.json:43` runs `uvicorn app.main:app … --reload --reload-dir app`.
- Today's logs show repeated graceful restarts: `Shutting down` → `Started server process` → `Application startup complete` at **19:02:52, 19:19:24, 19:20:19, 19:20:30**, and each cold start takes **~12 seconds** to become ready.
- The `/onboarding` page fires `integrations/status`, `integrations/config`, and `triggers/schema` concurrently on mount. When that burst lands inside a restart window, all three get `ERR_EMPTY_RESPONSE`.
- That's why **no `GET /triggers/schema` from your browser ever appears in the backend request log** — the request died before the server produced a response.

Why you see the toast for *trigger schemas* specifically: `apiService` only suppresses `401`s (`service.ts:60`). A network error is not a 401, so it shows the configured `errorMessage`. `getTriggerSchemas` passes `errorMessage: "Failed to fetch trigger schemas"` (`workflowApi.ts:301`), while `getIntegrationStatus` swallows its error and returns `{ integrations: [] }` (`integrationsApi.ts:76`). React Query's `retry: 2` (`QueryProvider.tsx:40`) turns one blip into a burst of toasts.

Two honest caveats:
- **Two of today's restarts (19:20:19, 19:20:30) were my fault** — I edited `app/core/middleware.py` during this session, which tripped `--reload`. I've reverted that file; it's back to original.
- The 19:19:24 restart was before my edits, so the server was already cycling while you tested (a backend `.py` save, or a manual restart).

## The fix

The transient backend unavailability during reload is just normal dev behavior. The actual bug worth fixing is that **a non-critical metadata fetch throws a scary error toast during onboarding**. `/triggers/schema` is only used to prettify trigger display, and the UI already degrades gracefully when it's missing (`findTriggerSchema` falls back to the raw slug, `utils.ts:67`). It shouldn't toast on failure — especially in the read-only onboarding preview.

Minimal fix: add `silent: true` to `getTriggerSchemas` (and optionally gate `useTriggerSchemas` so it doesn't fire while the modal is closed/in preview mode).

Want me to apply the `silent: true` change? I won't touch anything else without your go-ahead, given I already caused noise with the middleware edit.

content.text:
Done. 

- `apps/api/app/core/middleware.py` — fully reverted to original (empty diff), so my earlier CORS edit left no trace.
- `apps/web/src/features/workflows/api/workflowApi.ts` — `getTriggerSchemas` now uses `silent: true` instead of an error toast.

**What this fixes:** the "Failed to fetch trigger schemas" toast will no longer appear when the backend blips (e.g. mid `--reload` restart during onboarding). The trigger-schema data is non-critical display metadata, and the UI already falls back to the raw slug when it's missing, so suppressing the toast is safe — React Query still retries and populates the data once the server is back.

**What this does *not* change:** the underlying transient failures (`ERR_EMPTY_RESPONSE`) still happen whenever a request lands during a server restart — that's just normal `uvicorn --reload` behavior while backend files are being saved. If you're seeing the server cycle without editing backend code, that's worth a separate look, but it's not something to "fix" in the reload setup itself.

If you also want to avoid firing `/triggers/schema` at all during the read-only onboarding preview, I can gate `useTriggerSchemas` with `enabled: isOpen && mode !== "preview"` in `WorkflowModal` — say the word.

@router.get("/{platform}/connect", response_model=InitiatePlatformConnectResponse)
async def initiate_platform_connect(
    platform: str,
    current_user: Optional[dict] = Depends(get_current_user),
) -> InitiatePlatformConnectResponse:
    """Initiate platform connection via OAuth or manual instructions.

    Returns:
        - OAuth URL if credentials configured
        - Manual instructions if OAuth not configured
    """
    if not current_user:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=401, detail="Not authenticated")

    if not Platform.is_valid(platform):
        raise HTTPException(status_code=400, detail="Invalid platform")

    user_id = current_user.get("user_id")
    if not isinstance(user_id, str):
        raise ValueError("user_id must be a string")
    log.set(
        user={"id": user_id}, operation="initiate_platform_connect", platform=platform
    )

    # Discord OAuth flow
    if platform == "discord" and settings.DISCORD_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID:
        state = await create_oauth_state(
            user_id=user_id,
            redirect_path="/settings?section=linked-accounts",
            integration_id="discord",
        )

        auth_url = (
            f"https://discord.com/api/oauth2/authorize"
            f"?client_id={settings.DISCORD_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID}"
            f"&redirect_uri={quote(settings.DISCORD_OAUTH_REDIRECT_URI)}"
            f"&response_type=code"
            f"&scope=identify"
            f"&state={state}"
        )
        log.set(outcome="success", auth_type="oauth")
        return InitiatePlatformConnectResponse(
            auth_url=auth_url, auth_type="oauth", instructions=None, action_link=None
        )

    # Slack OAuth flow
    if platform == "slack" and settings.SLACK_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID:
        state = await create_oauth_state(
            user_id=user_id,
            redirect_path="/settings?section=linked-accounts",
            integration_id="slack",
        )

        auth_url = (
            f"https://slack.com/oauth/v2/authorize"
            f"?client_id={settings.SLACK_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID}"
            f"&redirect_uri={quote(settings.SLACK_OAUTH_REDIRECT_URI)}"
            f"&user_scope=identity.basic"
            f"&state={state}"
        )

content.text: Now both root causes are clear. Issue 2: `GET /platform-links/{platform}/connect` returns **501** at line 228 when a platform's OAuth isn't configured. The Slack branch (line 187) requires `settings.SLACK_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID`; if it's unset, it falls through to the 501, which the UI surfaces as "internal server error." Let me confirm the env var is missing and check how the connect button handles the error.

Issue 1 is fixed — `OnboardingChatStream` already supports `hideBotAvatar` (it forwards to `ChatBubbleBot`'s `hideAvatar`); the accordion call just wasn't passing it. Now the "Ran/Saved todos" accordion renders the run-now reply without the GAIA avatar.

## Issue 2: the Slack 501

**Why it errors:** Clicking Slack calls `GET /api/v1/platform-links/slack/connect`. That handler (`platform_links.py:187`) only returns a Slack OAuth URL if `settings.SLACK_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID` is set; otherwise it falls through to `raise HTTPException(status_code=501, "slack OAuth not configured")` (line 228). Your `apps/api/.env` has it **commented out**:
```
# SLACK_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID=...
# SLACK_OAUTH_CLIENT_SECRET=...
```
The gaia log confirms it: `GET /api/v1/platform-links/slack/connect … 501`. Same applies to Discord (also commented out). Telegram/WhatsApp work because they use a manual flow with no OAuth creds.

So this is **missing local config, not a code bug** — Slack OAuth isn't set up in your dev environment. To make the button actually connect, you'd set `SLACK_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID` / `SLACK_OAUTH_CLIENT_SECRET` (+ `SLACK_OAUTH_REDIRECT_URI`) in `.env`. I can't fill in real Slack credentials for you.

**Secondary UX bug worth noting:** the onboarding connect handler (`useConnectPlatform.ts`) calls the endpoint with `silent: true` and, on *any* failure, runs `finish()` — which dispatches `platformConnected`. So an unconfigured platform doesn't show a clean message; it either surfaces the raw error (depending on your running bundle) or **falsely marks the platform as "Connected."** That's the part actually worth fixing in code.

How do you want to handle #2?

questions.question: How should I handle the Slack/Discord connect issue in onboarding?

options.label: Graceful fail + don't false-connect
options.description: On a connect failure (e.g. 501 unconfigured), show a small 'Slack isn't available right now' toast and do NOT mark the platform connected. Keeps the picker honest in any environment.

options.label: Hide unconfigured platforms
options.description: Only show platforms whose OAuth/manual flow is actually available, so unconfigured ones (Slack/Discord locally) don't appear as clickable buttons at all.

options.label: Leave as-is (config only)
options.description: No code change. It's just missing local Slack OAuth creds; you'll set the env vars yourself when needed.

content.text:
Both fixes are in and Biome-clean.

## Summary

**1. GAIA avatar in the "Ran/Saved todos" accordion** — `CompletedStagesTimeline.tsx:111` now passes `hideBotAvatar` to `OnboardingChatStream`. The prop already existed and forwards to `ChatBubbleBot`'s `hideAvatar`; it just wasn't being set in the accordion. The run-now reply now renders without the avatar inside the accordion.

**2. Slack "internal server error"** — root cause was config: `SLACK_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID` is commented out in `apps/api/.env`, so `GET /platform-links/slack/connect` returns **501** (`platform_links.py:228`). Per your choice, I fixed the frontend handling in `useConnectPlatform.ts`: on any connect failure it now shows a gentle `"Slack isn't available to connect right now."` toast and **no longer falsely marks the platform as connected** (the old `catch { finish() }` was the bug). 

To make Slack actually connect in dev, you'll still need to set `SLACK_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID` / `SLACK_OAUTH_CLIENT_SECRET` / `SLACK_OAUTH_REDIRECT_URI` in `.env` — same for Discord. Telegram/WhatsApp don't need creds (manual flow), so those buttons keep working.

Changed files:
- `apps/web/src/features/onboarding/components/CompletedStagesTimeline.tsx`
- `apps/web/src/features/onboarding/hooks/useConnectPlatform.ts`
- `apps/web/src/features/workflows/api/workflowApi.ts` (the earlier `silent` fix)

content: Fixed the onboarding bugs you reported: the trigger-schema error toast, the GAIA avatar showing in the todos accordion, and the Slack connect failure (now graceful). Next step is yours to verify in the browser.
